resources.string: Learn the difference between val and var. Understand how to declare functions and use basic data types. This is the core foundation of all development.
Explore branching operators. The when operator in Kotlin is much more powerful and flexible than the traditional switch in other languages.
Kotlin protects you from NullPointerException. Study the ?. (safe call) and ?: (elvis operator) to handle null values gracefully.
Create your first classes. Learn how regular classes differ from data classes, which automatically generate equals and hashCode methods.
Understand the difference between mutable and immutable collections. This is critical for managing data within lists efficiently.
Understand how the element hierarchy works. Learn LinearLayout and FrameLayout — the simplest ways to arrange buttons and text.
This is the most powerful tool for building complex screens. Learn how to constrain elements to each other and the screen edges.
Understand what happens to the app when a user minimizes it or rotates the screen. This knowledge prevents data loss.
Use explicit intents to launch new Activities and implicit intents to open a browser or make a phone call.
Study Google\'s guidelines. Use MaterialCardView and proper padding to make your app look professional and modern.
Move all your colors to a separate file. This allows you to easily implement a dark theme for your app in the future.
Learn the difference between PNG and WebP formats. Always optimize image sizes to ensure your app doesn\'t consume too much memory.
Never write text sizes in dp. Use sp for text and dp for padding/margins so that the UI scales correctly for all users.
If content is larger than the screen, wrap it in a ScrollView. Remember, it can only contain one direct child element.
RecyclerView is a "recycler". It doesn\'t create thousands of views, but reuses those that have already scrolled off the screen.
The ViewHolder stores references to the UI elements. This saves the system from calling findViewById during every scroll.
The adapter takes your data list and turns it into visual cards. Implement getItemCount and onBindViewHolder methods.
Set an OnClickListener in your adapter so that users can navigate to the details of each specific tip.
Use cardElevation and cardCornerRadius to create a depth effect and make your tips feel tactile and premium.
Create a values-ru folder. This makes your app accessible to millions of additional users across the world.
Use Log.d() to track variables in real-time. It\'s much more effective than just guessing why the code isn\'t working.
Learn how to save simple data, such as a username or a theme preference, so it persists even after the app restarts.
Never perform heavy tasks on the main thread. Use coroutines to keep your app smooth and responsive without freezes.
Retrofit allows your app to communicate with servers. This is the industry standard for fetching data from the internet.
Data from the internet usually comes in JSON format. The Gson library helps convert it into Kotlin objects with one line of code.
Separate logic from the UI. ViewModel preserves data during rotation, while LiveData notifies the UI about updates.
Fragments allow you to build flexible interfaces that are easy to adapt for tablets and different screen sizes.
Room is a wrapper over SQLite. Use it to store large amounts of data locally on the user\'s device.
A good developer writes tests. Start with simple JUnit tests to ensure your business logic is working correctly.
Congratulations! You finished the 30-day journey. Now learn how to build the final app bundle to share with friends or the store.
